elements.sent-2411-001: Stability guarantees are crucial when ensuring that a fully autonomous robot does not take undesirable or potentially harmful actions.
elements.sent-2411-002: We recently proposed the Neural Contractive Dynamical Systems (NCDS), which is a neural network architecture that guarantees contractive stability.
elements.sent-2411-003: With this, learning-from-demonstrations approaches can trivially provide stability guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, our early work left several unanswered questions, which we here address.
elements.sent-2411-005: Beyond providing an in-depth explanation of NCDS, this paper extends the framework with more careful regularization, a conditional variant of the framework for handling multiple tasks, and an uncertainty-driven approach to latent obstacle avoidance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments verify that the developed system has the flexibility of ordinary neural networks while providing the stability guarantees needed for autonomous robotics.
elements.sent-2411-007: Autonomous robotic systems require stability guarantees to ensure safe and reliable operation, especially in dynamic and unpredictable environments.
elements.sent-2411-008: Manually designed robot movements can achieve such stability guarantees, but even skilled engineers struggle to hand-code highly dynamic motions~\citep{Billard16 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In contrast, learning robot skills from human demonstrations is an efficient and intuitive approach for encoding highly dynamic motions into a robot's repertoire \citep{Schaal03 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Unfortunately, learning-based approaches often struggle to ensure stability as they rely on the machine learning model to extrapolate in a controlled manner.
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, controlling the extrapolation behavior of neural networks has proven challenging~\citep{xu2020neural , which hampers stability guarantees.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Many tasks require the robot to dynamically follow desired trajectories, e.g.\@ in flexible manufacturing, human-robot interaction, or entertainment settings.
elements.sent-2411-013: In these cases, asymptotic stability, which guarantees stability only with respect to a fixed point~\citep{EuclidFlow2020:Rana, KhansariZadeh2011:StableEstimatorDS,zhang2022RiemannianStableDS , is insufficient, demanding a more general stability concept.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Contraction theory~\citep{Lohmiller1998ContractionAnalysis, Bullo2024:ContractionBook is particularly well-suited, as it ensures that all path integrals, regardless of their initial state, incrementally converge over time.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Unfortunately, the mathematical requirements of a contractive system are difficult to ensure in popular neural network architectures.\looseness=-1 To address this problem, our previous work introduced Neural Contractive Dynamical Systems (NCDS, \citet{NCDS:2023BeikMohammadi ), a neural network architecture designed to ensure contractive behavior across all parameter values.}
elements.sent-2411-016: This model enables robots to maintain stability even when subjected to external perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-017: The original NCDS (``vanilla NCDS'') framework provides a solid foundation for learning complex contractive dynamical systems.
elements.sent-2411-018: Still, there remains potential for further refinement, particularly in formulating the Jacobian of the learned system dynamics, which is crucial for stability and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-019: Vanilla NCDS employs a symmetric Jacobian with a constant regularization term to enforce contraction~\citep{Lohmiller1998ContractionAnalysis, Jouffroy2010ConractiontheoryTutorial .}
elements.sent-2411-020: While effective, this approach left room for exploring how different regularization methods and Jacobian formulations could enhance the model's performance both within and beyond the data support region.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{Sections/Plots/Regularization/Teaser_new_IJRR_11_Green.png} \caption{Robot motion generated for a grasping skill using a Conditional Neural Contractive Dynamical System (CNCDS). The robot motion is conditioned on an input image of the object placed on a desk. CNCDS also learns a Riemannian manifold from demonstrations, representing a safety region to navigate through.\looseness=-1} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-021: The vanilla NCDS framework learns high-dimensional dynamics via a low-dimensional contractive latent space.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, it performs all obstacle avoidance computations in the ambient (high-dimensional) space, which may be computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-023: To the best of our knowledge, no existing method for learning dynamical systems can simultaneously learn contractive stable dynamics while also performing obstacle avoidance in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-024: To achieve this, we extend the work of \citet{BeikMohammadi23:ReactiveMotion , and leverage a Riemannian pullback metric, derived from a VAE injective decoder, to lift the obstacle avoidance problem to the latent space.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This ensures the avoidance of unsafe regions outside of data support.
elements.sent-2411-026: On the multi-task front, the vanilla NCDS framework, like most methods that learn contractive dynamical systems ~\citep{Tsukamoto2021NeuralContractionMetric, Dawson2022LearnedCertificates, jaffe2024:ELCD , was designed to learn a single skill.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Although this may suffice for certain tasks, a more general approach would involve enabling the learning of multiple skills within the same contractive dynamical system.
elements.sent-2411-028: This reduces not only the use of computational resources but also the need to train separate models for each skill.\looseness=-1 In this paper, we introduce several advancements to the vanilla NCDS aimed at improving the model's flexibility and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our first contribution focuses on the regularization of the Jacobian of the learned system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-030: We investigate various unexplored strategies, including state-independent and state-dependent regularization vectors, as well as eigenvalue-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-031: These methods aim to fine-tune the contraction properties of the system, leading to more robust generalization and faster convergence within the data region.
elements.sent-2411-032: Our second contribution explores the potential benefits of introducing asymmetry into the system Jacobian matrix by incorporating both symmetric and skew-symmetric components.
elements.sent-2411-033: This asymmetry aims at increasing the model's flexibility, allowing it to capture more complex dynamical behaviors~\citep{jaffe2024:ELCD .}
elements.sent-2411-034: This investigation builds on the understanding that, while symmetry in the Jacobian provides certain stability benefits, asymmetry could offer more nuanced control over the system's dynamics, particularly relevant in complex dynamic skills.
elements.sent-2411-035: Our third contribution, ``conditional NCDS'' (CNCDS), extends the model to handle multiple motion skills by conditioning on task-related variables such as target states.
elements.sent-2411-036: This conditional framework allows a single NCDS module to adapt to varying task conditions, broadening its applicability in more complex, multimodal robotic tasks.
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, our fourth contribution extends NCDS with latent obstacle avoidance capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our approach, inspired by modulation matrix techniques, allows the system to navigate around dynamic obstacles while maintaining contractive stability \citep{Huber2022ModulationMatrix, Huber2019:ConvexConcaveObstacles .}
elements.sent-2411-039: We frame obstacle avoidance from a Riemannian perspective and define safety regions in the latent space through Riemannian pullback metrics~\citep{BeikMohammadi23:ReactiveMotion .}
elements.sent-2411-040: This designates both obstacles and out-of-data-support regions as unsafe.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this section, we revisit the neural contractive dynamical system (NCDS) method introduced by~\cite{NCDS:2023BeikMohammadi .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Later, we explain how NCDS can be improved through the introduction of a new data-driven regularization technique and modifications to the symmetry of its Jacobian formulation.
elements.sent-2411-043: Our main goal is to design a flexible neural architecture that is guaranteed to always output a contractive vector field.
elements.sent-2411-044: First, we introduce contraction theory as it is prerequisite to our design.
elements.sent-2411-045: Assume an autonomous dynamical system $\dot{\x _t = f(\x_t)$, where $\x_t \in \R^D$ is the state variable, $f: \R^D \rightarrow \R^D$ is a $C^1$ function, and $\dot{\x _t = \nicefrac{\mathrm{d}\x {\mathrm{d t}$ denotes temporal differentiation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: As depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:example , contraction stability guarantees that all solution trajectories of a nonlinear system $f$ incrementally converge regardless of initial conditions $\x_0,\dot{\x _0$, and temporary perturbations \citep{Lohmiller1998ContractionAnalysis .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The system stability can, thus, be analyzed differentially, i.e.\@ we can ask if two nearby trajectories converge to one another.
elements.sent-2411-048: Specifically, contraction theory defines a measure of distance between neighboring trajectories, known as the contraction metric, under which the distance decreases exponentially over time \citep{Jouffroy2010ConractiontheoryTutorial, Tsukamoto2021TutorialContraction .}

elements.sent-2411-059: Note that it is not trivial to impose a negative definiteness constraint on a network's Jacobian without compromising its expressiveness.
elements.sent-2411-060: To overcome this challenge, we first design a neural network $\hat{\Jac_f}$ representing directly the Jacobian of our final network.
elements.sent-2411-061: This produces matrix-valued negative definite outputs.
elements.sent-2411-062: The final neural network, parametrizing $f_{\bm{\theta }$, will then be formed by integrating the Jacobian network.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Background: deep generative models and Riemannian manifolds
elements.sent-2411-191: To obtain a low-dimensional representation of the demonstration data, the vanilla NCDS leverages Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) to encode high-dimensional vector fields into a low-dimensional latent space.

elements.sent-2411-198: VAEs are generative models~\citep{kingma:autoencoding that learn and reconstruct data by encapsulating their density into a lower-dimensional latent space $\latent$.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Specifically, VAEs approximate the training data density $\Prob(\x)$ in an ambient space $\ambient$ through a low-dimensional latent variable $\z$.
elements.sent-2411-200: For simplicity, we consider the generative process of a Gaussian VAE defined as, \begin{equation}\Prob(\z) = N\left(\z | 0, \I_d\right), \z \in \latent ; \Prob_{\varrho}(\x|\z) = N\left(\z|\mu_{\varrho}(\z), \I_D \sigma_{\varrho}^2(\z)\right), \x \in \ambient. \label{eq:vae_gen}\end{equation} where $\mu_{\bm{\varrho : \latent \rightarrow \ambient$ and $\sigma_{\bm{\varrho : \latent \rightarrow \R^D_+$ are deep neural networks with parameters $\bm{\varrho}$ estimating the mean and the variance of the posterior distribution $\Prob_{\bm{\varrho (\x|\z)$, and $\I_D$ and $\I_d$ are identity matrices of size $D$ and $d$, respectively.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-209: In differential geometry, Riemannian manifolds are referred to as curved $d$-dimensional continuous and differentiable surfaces characterized by a Riemannian metric~\citep{Lee18Riemann .}
elements.sent-2411-210: This metric is characterized by a family of smoothly varying positive-definite inner products acting on the tangent spaces of the manifold, which locally resembles the Euclidean space $\R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-211: In this paper, we use the mapping function $\Omega$ to represent a manifold $\Manifold$ immersed in the ambient space $\ambient$ defined as, \begin{equation}\Manifold = \Omega(\latent) \quad with \quad \Omega: \latent \rightarrow \ambient, \label{eq:mapping}\end{equation} where $\latent$ and $\ambient$ are open subsets of Euclidean spaces with $\dim{\latent < \dim{\ambient}$.}
elements.sent-2411-212: An important operation on Riemannian manifolds is the computation of the length of a smooth curve $\zeta: [0, 1] \rightarrow \latent$, \begin{equation}\Length_{\zeta} = \int_0^1 \| \partial_t \Omega(\zeta(t)) \| dt . \label{eq:length}\end{equation} This length can be reformulated using the chain rule as, \begin{equation}\Length_{\zeta} = \int_0^1 \sqrt{\dot{\zeta}(t)^{\trsp} \Metric(\zeta(t)) \dot{\zeta}(t)} dt, \label{eq:length_chain_rule}\end{equation} where $\Metric$ and $\dot{\zeta _t = \partial_t \zeta$ are the Riemannian metric and curve derivative, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-213: Note that the Riemannian metric corresponds to, \begin{equation}\Metric(\z) = \Jac_{\Omega}(\z)^{\trsp} \Jac_{\Omega}(\z) . \label{eq:RiemMetric}\end{equation} Here, $\Jac_{\Omega (\z)$ is the Jacobian of the mapping function $\Omega$.}
elements.sent-2411-214: This metric can be used to measure local distances in $\latent$.
elements.sent-2411-215: The shortest path on the manifold, also known as the geodesic, can be computed given the curve length in~\eqref{eq:length_chain_rule .}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Task-Space Training Using $\SO$} \label{alg:Training} \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{Data: $\tau_n = \left \{\x_{n, t}, \bm{R}_{n, t} \right \}_{n=1}^N, \quad t \in [1, T_n]$} \KwOut{Learned contractive dynamics parameters $\bm{\theta}$.} \ForEach{trajectory $n$}{ \ForEach{time step $t$}{ $\bm{r}_{n,t} = \operatorname{Log}(\bm{R}_{n,t})$ \Comment{Extract skew-sym coeffs} $\bm{p}_{n,t} = [\x_{n,t}, \bm{r}_{n,t}]$ \Comment{Form new state vector} } } $\bm{\xi}^* = \arg \min_{\bm{\xi}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}(\bm{p}_{n,t})$ \Comment{Train the VAE} \ForEach{trajectory $n$}{ \ForEach{time step $t$}{ $\z_{n,t} = \mu_{\bm{\xi}^*}(\bm{p}_{n,t})$ \Comment{Encode poses} $\dot{\z}_{n,t} = \frac{\z_{n,t+1} - \z_{n,t}}{\Delta t}$ \Comment{Compute latent velocities} } } $\bm{\theta}^* = \arg \min_{\bm{\theta}} \mathcal{L}_{\text{Jac}}(\bm{p}_{n, t})$ \Comment{Train Jacobian network}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Robot Control Scheme} \label{alg:robot_control} \SetAlgoLined \KwIn{Data: $[\x_t, \bm{R}_t]$ \Comment{Current state of the robot}} \KwOut{$\dot{\x}_t$ \Comment{Velocity of the end-effector}} \ForEach{time step $t$}{ $\bm{r}_{t} = \operatorname{Log}(\bm{R}_{t})$ \Comment{Extract skew-sym coeffs} $\bm{p}_{t} = [\x_{t}, \bm{r}_{t}]$ \Comment{Form new state vector} $\z_t = \mu_{\bm{\xi}}(\bm{p}_t)$ \Comment{Compute the latent state} $\hat{\dot{\z}}_t = f(\z_t)$ \Comment{Compute the latent velocity} $\bm{J}_{\mu_{\bm{\xi}}}(\z_t) = \frac{\partial \mu_{\bm{\xi}}}{\partial \z_t}$ \Comment{Compute the Jacobian of the decoder} $\dot{\x}_t = \bm{J}_{\mu_{\bm{\xi}}}(\z_t)\hat{\dot{\z}}_t$ \Comment{Compute input space velocity} }

elements.sent-2411-488: In this paper, we proposed an enhanced framework for designing neural networks that are contractive, building upon the original Neural Contractive Dynamical Systems (NCDS, \cite{NCDS:2023BeikMohammadi ).}
elements.sent-2411-489: This allows us to create a flexible class of models capable of learning dynamical systems from demonstrations while retaining contractive stability guarantees, offering a more controlled generalization behavior.
elements.sent-2411-490: Key improvements include the introduction of Jacobian matrix regularization, where we explore both state-independent and state-dependent regularization strategies, as well as eigenvalue-based methods, to fine-tune the contraction properties and improve robustness and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-491: Furthermore, we extended the NCDS framework by introducing asymmetry into the Jacobian matrix, incorporating both symmetric and skew-symmetric components.
elements.sent-2411-492: However, our evaluation showed that this modification did not provide any significant advantage over using only the symmetric part.
elements.sent-2411-493: This suggests that the NCDS with symmetric Jacobian is sufficient to locally approximate the behavior of dynamical systems with non-symmetric Jacobians, eliminating the need for the added complexity.
elements.sent-2411-494: We also introduced the Conditional NCDS (CNCDS), which allows us to handle multiple motion skills by conditioning on task-related variables, such as target states, allowing a single NCDS module to adapt to multiple task conditions.
elements.sent-2411-495: Inspired by modulation matrix techniques, this approach reshapes the latent learned vector field near unsafe areas, enabling the system to avoid obstacles and out-of-data support regions, while maintaining contractive stability guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-496: Overall, our extended NCDS framework significantly addresses the key limitations of the original model, introducing improvements in flexibility, robustness, and adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-498: The model remains sensitive to the choice of numerical integration schemes, particularly the reliance on adaptive step sizing to ensure reliable performance, which increases computation time and poses challenges for real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-499: Moreover, the need to compute the Jacobian of the decoder at every step, in order to calculate the ambient velocity and Riemannian metric, further impacts performance, adding computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-500: Furthermore, a limitation of implementing the conditional variable in CNCDS is its limited reactivity to changes in the conditioning variable.
elements.sent-2411-501: In other words, if the conditioning variable changes during skill execution, it is uncertain whether CNCDS will adapt seamlessly to these changes.
elements.sent-2411-502: We consider these issues minor compared to the many benefits that the NCDS framework provides.

elements.sent-2411-638: In our framework, although the latent and ambient spaces share the same overall dimensionality due to the padding operation, the encoder projects data into a lower-dimensional representation by discarding certain dimensions (via unpadding).
elements.sent-2411-639: These discarded (or extra) dimensions, while not directly used in the final projection, retain information that is indicative of whether a point lies on the decoder’s manifold.
elements.sent-2411-640: We define the decoder’s manifold as the set of latent points for which the extra dimensions are exactly zero.
elements.sent-2411-641: Formally, let $\z=[\z_m,\z_e]$ be the latent vector, where $\z_m$ represents the dimensions used for the final projection and $\z_e$ the extra dimensions, then a point is considered on the decoder's manifold if $\parallel \! \z_e \! \parallel = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-642: Conversely, any nonzero values in $\z_e$ indicate that the corresponding state lies outside the decoder’s manifold.
elements.sent-2411-643: Although deviations from the decoder’s manifold are generally minimal, in certain real-robot scenarios measurement noise or ambient perturbations can occasionally cause the latent representation to stray from the decoder's manifold.
elements.sent-2411-644: In these cases, our approach incorporates a transition phase to correct the deviation.
elements.sent-2411-645: Specifically, if a latent point is identified as being off the manifold, we compute a transition vector that incrementally steers the point back toward the manifold.
elements.sent-2411-646: This is achieved by integrating along a path in latent space until the condition $\parallel \! \z_e \! \parallel \simeq 0$ is effectively reached, thereby ensuring compatibility with the decoder’s assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-647: This corrective mechanism is not invoked routinely but serves as an important safeguard to enhance system robustness in the rare instances when deviations occur.

elements.subsection-2411-019: Eigenvalue metric maps of symmetric vs. asymmetric Jacobian
elements.sent-2411-672: Figure~\ref{fig:assymetric_contraction_metric_heatmaps shows different eigenvalue metric heatmaps of the learned contractive dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-673: The top row shows that symmetric Jacobian approach results in increased contraction along the ``dominant'' eigenvector.
elements.sent-2411-674: In the middle row, we observe that even the ``non-dominant'' eigenvector has stronger contraction under this method.
elements.sent-2411-675: Finally, the bottom row shows a higher overall contraction spread, indicating a more pronounced pull toward a specific eigenvector.
elements.sent-2411-676: More precisely, in the Angle dataset, the average minimum eigenvalue is $-0.266$ compared to $-0.046$ for the asymmetric case, while the average maximum eigenvalue remains more negative ($-0.034$ vs. $-0.006$).
elements.sent-2411-677: The maximum contraction spread, representing the difference between these eigenvalues, is significantly higher for the symmetric model ($2.489$ vs. $0.362$), confirming its more robust contractive behavior.
elements.sent-2411-678: Extreme values further support this trend, with the minimum eigenvalue reaching $-2.589$ for the symmetric case, compared to only $-0.388$ for the asymmetric model.
elements.sent-2411-679: Similarly, in the Sine dataset, the symmetric model maintains a average minimum eigenvalue of $-0.381$ versus $-0.063$ in the asymmetric case, with a more negative average maximum eigenvalue ($-0.042$ vs. $-0.003$).
elements.sent-2411-680: The maximum contraction spread is also notably higher ($1.457$ vs. $0.439$), reinforcing the role of symmetry in promoting directional contraction.
elements.sent-2411-681: The extreme minimum eigenvalue reaches $-1.489$ in the symmetric formulation, whereas it remains at $-0.439$ for the asymmetric model.
